pattern: Because the two houses that mirror each other around the middle must differ in color just like true neighbors do, folding the row in half and tracking a state of (color of house i, color of house n-1-i) turns the row into a short chain of paired decisions solvable with a small dynamic program.
hints: Instead of thinking about n separate houses, pair up house i with its mirror house n-1-i for i from 0 up to n/2-1 -- each pair must use two different colors from one another.

Track a dynamic-programming state that is the pair of colors used for the current pair of houses, and only allow transitioning from a previous pair's colors when neither color repeats against its own neighbor (house i against house i-1, and house n-1-i against house n-i).

At the very first pair there is no previous neighbor to worry about, so every pair of distinct colors is a valid starting state; the answer is the minimum total cost over all valid color-pair states at the last pair.

edge_inputs.description: smallest possible row of two houses
four houses with clearly distinct color costs
all costs at zero, any valid coloring is free
all costs at the maximum bound
one color is always cheapest, but reusing it on a mirrored pair is forbidden and forces a costlier choice
adjacent-only reasoning would wrongly allow the two middle houses to match
eight houses with varied costs per position

stress.description: random even-length rows with random small costs
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 2000))) * 2
    cost = [[rng.randint(0, 100000) for _ in range(3)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [n, cost]
